title: The shell pattern, fan curves on a stone-dropped base
subtitle: How to produce the concentric fan motif using stylus semicircle passes
excerpt: The shell pattern is one of the most visually complex of the traditional marbled-paper designs: a stylus is drawn in repeating semicircle passes across a stone-dropped base, producing nested concentric fans that read as overlapping shells across the full sheet. It requires precise control of arc width and spacing.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894): Halfer classifies the shell as among the most demanding of the classical patterns, requiring a clean stone base and highly controlled stylus motion. C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881) provides an earlier account of the shell family.

glossaryTerms.definition: The simplest base pattern in marbling: pigments are dropped onto the size in alternating colours without any raking or combing, producing an irregular stone-like field of rings.
The shell pattern's defining motion: the stylus tip is drawn in repeated flat U-shapes across the tray, each pass offset from the last to produce nested fan curves.

content.text: as its base rather than a combed nonpareil. The stone base preserves more pigment volume in each ring, which the
passes deform into arcs. The precision required is high: the semicircles must be consistent in radius and equally spaced, or the nested fan effect collapses into an irregular smear.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry safety
attrs.body: Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying it to paper and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is very slippery, wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

items.name: Carrageenan (marbling size)
items.notes: Prepared 24 hours ahead. For shell pattern work, a slightly firmer size (2% carrageenan) holds the stone drops more crisply before the stylus pass.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid
items.notes: Three to four strongly contrasting colours work better than many similar ones for shell pattern.

items.name: Ox-gall (marbling)
items.notes: Critical: each colour needs careful ox-gall adjustment so the drops stay as distinct rings without merging before the stylus pass.

items.name: Alum, potash (mordant)
items.notes: Applied to paper and fully dried.

items.name: Marbling tray
items.notes: At least the size of the sheet. Straight, rigid sides are helpful for judging semicircle consistency.

items.name: Marbling stylus
items.notes: The tip must be smooth, a rough or damaged tip tears the pigment rather than pulling it cleanly.

content.text: Drop the pigments in a regular pattern of alternating colours across the full size surface without any raking. This builds the
base. Aim for even ring sizes and full surface coverage. Larger drops with moderate ox-gall produce the best base for shell work.

Position the stylus at the top left corner of the tray, tip resting on the size surface. Draw a smooth flat U-shape from left to right, the U should just touch the tray's bottom edge at its lowest point. Lift and reposition to the right of the first pass by the same width as the U. Draw the next semicircle in the same direction. Repeat across the full tray. For a double-shell effect, return with a second row of semicircles offset by half a U width. These
passes nest the deformed rings into the fan motif.

Hold the alum-treated sheet by its two short edges and lower it onto the size centre-first, releasing both edges simultaneously. Allow ten to fifteen seconds of contact, then peel one long edge back in a smooth motion. Rinse under running water immediately and lay flat to dry.

items.problem: Semicircles are not consistent in width
items.solution: Use the tray edges as visual guides and count passes. Mark the top of the tray with small pencil marks at the intended spacing before starting the stylus pass.

items.problem: Stone drops have merged before the stylus pass
items.solution: The ox-gall ratio is too high or the size is too warm. Reduce ox-gall and allow the size to reach room temperature before use.

items.problem: Stylus tears the pigment instead of deforming it smoothly
items.solution: The stylus tip is damaged or the pass speed is too fast. Slow the motion and check the tip under magnification for burrs.
